_description: Structured semantic contracts for support actions. Action IDs are aligned to knowledge/call_mix_library.json.
_note: Use hard filters for forbidden_structures and requires_context before applying score adjustments. source_library keeps the original library hints.

keepspace.description: Do nothing actively. Let the audience listen to the music without any call or action. This is not a failure — it's an intentional choice to let the song's own energy carry the moment.
keepspace.when_to_use: Verses, bridges, or any section where the lyrics or instrumental performance deserve full attention. Default action when nothing else fits.
keepspace.when_not_to_use: Choruses, especially the final chorus. Long instrumental breaks where the audience expects participation.
notes: In pre-chorus and pre-chorus_build, keep space is allowed only as a fallback for dense vocals, restrained songs, or missing context; prefer MIX/rhythm calls for momentum.

standard_mix.description: The classic Japanese idol/anisong MIX chant pattern. A rapid-fire spoken chant that the audience performs during instrumental breaks. The MIX has a standard template (Aaa~ MIX! ...) known across the entire culture.
standard_mix.when_to_use: Instrumental breaks (4-8 bars is standard). Intro if the song has a well-known intro MIX.
standard_mix.when_not_to_use: Verses (clashes with lyrics). Pre-chorus (disrupts build-up). Outro. Short instrumental breaks under 2 bars.

source_library.typical_text: Aaa, yossha ikuzo! Tiger! Fire! Cyber! Fiber! Diver! Viber! Jya Jya!

notes: Allowed in solo sections when the beat grid and bar count are clear; keep existing duration, risk, and context requirements.

standard_mix_long.description: Same as Standard MIX but with an extra 1 bar added. Used when the instrumental break has a clear 5-bar slot.
standard_mix_long.when_not_to_use: Same constraints as Standard MIX. Short instrumental breaks.

source_library.typical_text: Standard MIX long phrase; fill exact chant text after local validation.

notes: Allowed in solo sections when the beat grid and bar count are clear; keep existing duration, risk, and context requirements.

mix_leadin_aaa_ikuzo.description: A short MIX lead-in chant, often 'Aaa~ Ikuzo!' used to kick off a MIX or signal transition into a high-energy section.
mix_leadin_aaa_ikuzo.when_to_use: Very short instrumental breaks (1-2 bars). Transition into the first MIX of a song.

source_library.typical_text: Aaa, yossha ikuzo!

notes: Allowed in solo sections when the beat grid and bar count are clear; keep existing duration, risk, and context requirements.

standard_mix_first_half.description: Only the first half of a Standard MIX. Used when the instrumental break is shorter than a full MIX allows.
standard_mix_first_half.when_to_use: Short instrumental breaks (3-4 bars). Transitional instrumental sections.
standard_mix_first_half.when_not_to_use: Full-length instrumental breaks where a complete MIX fits.

source_library.typical_text: Aaa, yossha ikuzo! Tiger! Fire! Cyber! Fiber!

notes: Allowed in solo sections when the beat grid and bar count are clear; keep existing duration, risk, and context requirements.

standard_mix_second_half.description: Standard MIX Second Half is a mix action from the support library. Use it where the source context hints and duration requirements match; avoid using it as a generic filler.
standard_mix_second_half.when_to_use: Best when section semantics, bar length, vocal density, and cultural context all match the source hints.
standard_mix_second_half.when_not_to_use: Avoid when vocals are dense, bar length does not match, or the action requires missing cultural/song context.

source_library.typical_text: Tiger! Fire! Cyber! Fiber! Diver! Viber! Jya! Jya!

std_mix_kouhan_dt.display_name: Standard MIX Second Half Double Time
std_mix_kouhan_dt.description: The second half of a Standard MIX performed at double speed. High-intensity.
std_mix_kouhan_dt.when_to_use: End of long instrumental breaks. Outro of high-energy songs.
std_mix_kouhan_dt.when_not_to_use: Medium or slow tempo sections. Verses, dense vocal lines, bridges, and pre-chorus moments without a clear 1-bar high-tension gap.

source_library.typical_text: Standard MIX second-half phrase performed at double speed.

notes: Allowed in solo sections when the beat grid and bar count are clear; keep existing duration, risk, and context requirements.

ainu_second_half_mix.description: The second-half segment of an Ainu-style MIX pattern. Ainu MIX is a specific call tradition originating from Hokkaido Ainu cultural patterns adapted into idol call culture.
ainu_second_half_mix.when_to_use: Long instrumental breaks (8+ bars) when the audience has already completed the first half.
ainu_second_half_mix.when_not_to_use: First MIX of a song. Short breaks. Culturally sensitive — use only when the audience demographic can be expected to know this pattern.

source_library.typical_text: Ainu MIX second-half phrase in romanized form.

notes: Pre-chorus/build-compatible when bar fit, vocal gap, beat stability, and cultural/live context all match.
Allowed in solo sections when the beat grid and bar count are clear; keep existing duration, risk, and context requirements.

ainu_kahen_mix.description: A flexible Ainu-style MIX that can be adapted to different bar lengths. More adaptable than standard Ainu MIX.
ainu_kahen_mix.when_to_use: Instrumental breaks or outros where a variable-length MIX is needed.
ainu_kahen_mix.when_not_to_use: Same cultural sensitivity notes as Ainu MIX Second Half.

source_library.typical_text: Ainu variable MIX phrase in romanized form.

notes: Screenshot placement: Ainu variable MIX is a short interlude before the later chorus block.

chokokoro_call.description: A call based on 'Choco Cornet' (chocolate horn pastry), strongly associated with Yamabuki Saaya (Saya) from BanG Dream! Poppin'Party. The call references Saya's family bakery and her love of chocolate cornets.
chokokoro_call.when_to_use: Verses in BanG Dream! Poppin'Party songs, especially Saya-focused songs. Character-specific call — verify character context before assigning.
chokokoro_call.when_not_to_use: Non-BanG Dream! songs. Songs from other franchises. Non-Saya character songs within BanG Dream!.

source_library.typical_text: Chokokoro call phrase; fill exact chant text after local validation.

notes: Strongly tied to Saaya/chocolate cornet context. Never use as a generic cute call.

jyajya_mix.description: A singer-specific MIX associated with Liyuu. Do not treat it as a generic Jya Jya / guitar-flavored MIX.
jyajya_mix.when_to_use: Only for Liyuu songs or live contexts where this MIX is part of the accepted call tradition.
jyajya_mix.when_not_to_use: Non-Liyuu songs, even if the section is rock-style, guitar-heavy, or technically fits the bar length.

source_library.typical_text: Jya Jya MIX phrase; fill exact chant text after local validation.

notes: Liyuu-specific MIX. Keep it out of generic pre-chorus/build filler unless metadata explicitly identifies a Liyuu song or accepted Liyuu call tradition.

bismarck+kanzenseniki_mix.description: A longer, more complex MIX combining 'Bismarck' and 'Kanzenseniki' patterns. More elaborate than standard MIX. Named after warship references common in anisong call culture.
bismarck+kanzenseniki_mix.when_to_use: Long instrumental breaks (8+ bars). Songs with military/marching themes. When the audience expects something beyond standard MIX.
bismarck+kanzenseniki_mix.when_not_to_use: Short breaks. Soft/gentle songs. Pre-chorus/build sections without enough bar length, beat stability, or tradition for a complex MIX.

gozen_ichiban_mix.description: A MIX pattern themed around 'number one in the morning'. Has a distinct rhythmic feel and specific call phrases. Less common than standard MIX or Jya Jya MIX.
gozen_ichiban_mix.when_to_use: Bridge sections. Specific songs where this MIX pattern is traditionally used.
gozen_ichiban_mix.when_not_to_use: General-purpose allocation. Songs where this MIX is not culturally established.

gongfu_she.description: An underground gei inspired by kung-fu movie gestures. Involves rapid hand movements and poses. Very high energy.
gongfu_she.when_to_use: Final choruses. Extended instrumental breaks. Maximum energy moments only.
gongfu_she.when_not_to_use: Same constraints as other underground gei. Never allocate to non-climax sections.

source_library.typical_text: Gongfu She underground-gei movement sequence.

notes: Large body-motion action. Requires experienced audience and enough physical/temporal space.

ali_shizi.description: An underground gei involving hand gestures mimicking lion/dragon movements. Visually impressive but physically demanding.
ali_shizi.when_to_use: Long instrumental breaks in high-energy songs. Final choruses of epic-feeling songs.
ali_shizi.when_not_to_use: Same constraints as other underground gei. Never in intro/verse/pre-chorus/bridge/outro.

source_library.typical_text: Ali Shizi underground-gei movement sequence.

notes: Large body-motion action. Requires experienced audience and enough physical/temporal space.

dian_bo_she.description: A complex underground gei (fan-developed performance art) involving rhythmic body movements and coordinated chants. 'Dian Bo She' involves a pointing/shooting gesture pattern. High difficulty — not for casual audiences.
dian_bo_she.when_to_use: Long chorus sections (final chorus preferred). Songs with strong, steady beats suitable for coordinated gei. Audience must be experienced.
dian_bo_she.when_not_to_use: Intros, verses, pre-chorus, bridges, outros. Casual or beginner-oriented contexts. Songs where the gei doesn't match the beat pattern.

source_library.typical_text: Dian Bo She underground-gei movement sequence.

notes: Large body-motion action. Requires experienced audience and enough physical/temporal space.

tian_qu_long.description: An underground gei with a 'dragon soaring to heaven' theme. Elaborate choreography. One of the most visually complex gei.
tian_qu_long.when_to_use: Very long instrumental breaks (16+ bars). Only for experienced audience contexts.
tian_qu_long.when_not_to_use: Same constraints as other underground gei. Especially never in short sections.

source_library.typical_text: Tian Qu underground-gei movement sequence, long 10 eight-count version.

notes: Large body-motion action. Requires experienced audience and enough physical/temporal space.

ppph.description: A call pattern alternating between clapping and shouting, following the rhythm 'Pan! Pa-Pan! Hyu~!' Very common in idol/anisong live culture.
ppph.when_to_use: Verses and instrumental breaks in high-energy songs. Universal enough for most idol/anisong contexts.
ppph.when_not_to_use: Ballads or very slow songs. Dense or fragile build-ups where the call would cover the ramp instead of supporting it.

source_library.typical_text: Pan-pa-pan, hyu!

fuwa_fuwa.description: A soft, floating call where the audience sways and chants 'Fuwa Fuwa' following the gentle rhythm. Has a dreamy, floating quality.
fuwa_fuwa.when_to_use: Chorus sections of softer, more melodic songs. Songs with 'fuwa fuwa' aesthetic.
fuwa_fuwa.when_not_to_use: Hard rock or intense songs. Pre-chorus build-up.

source_library.typical_text: Fuwa fuwa!

fufu_call.description: A short rhythmic 'Fu! Fu! Fu!' call following the beat. Quick and punchy.
fufu_call.when_to_use: Pre-chorus build-up (as a simple rhythmic pulse). Short instrumental breaks.
fufu_call.when_not_to_use: Verses where lyrics need focus. Outro.

source_library.typical_text: Fu! Fu! Fu!

hai_hai.description: A universal call-and-response where the performer says 'Hai!' and the audience repeats 'Hai!' in rhythm. Simple, iconic, works everywhere.
hai_hai.when_to_use: Chorus openings, simple high-energy response slots, and light verse/pre-chorus fill when the vocal line leaves enough rhythmic room.
hai_hai.when_not_to_use: Quiet sections, fragile lyric moments, or dense vocals where repeated response calls would cover the singer.

source_library.typical_text: Hai! Hai!

notes: Can serve as a generic 'military call' style filler in verse/pre-chorus; keep it sparse around dense vocals.

o_call.description: A universal opening call where the audience shouts a sustained 'Ooooooo~!' following the vocal melody. Signals recognition of the song and builds anticipation.
o_call.when_to_use: Intro of any song. The first thing the audience does when recognizing a song.
o_call.when_not_to_use: Mid-song. Verses or choruses. After the song is already established.

source_library.typical_text: O! O! O! O!

oi_oi.description: The most basic call: 'Oi! Oi!' shouted in rhythm. The default go-to when nothing fancier is needed.
oi_oi.when_to_use: Instrumental breaks, rock chorus pulses, and sparse verse/pre-chorus call slots where a simple military-call feel is wanted.

source_library.typical_text: Oi! Oi!

notes: Can serve as a generic 'military call' style filler in verse/pre-chorus; prefer short sparse placements over full-section repetition.

clap.description: Simple hand clapping following the beat. The most universal participatory action.
clap.when_to_use: Anywhere that needs a gentle, universal rhythm marker. Bridges, transitional sections.
clap.when_not_to_use: Sections where clapping would clash with the music's natural rhythm.

source_library.typical_text: Clap on beat or backbeat.

name_call.description: Name Call is a rhythmcall action from the support library. Use it where the source context hints and duration requirements match; avoid using it as a generic filler.
name_call.when_to_use: Best when section semantics, bar length, vocal density, and cultural context all match the source hints.
name_call.when_not_to_use: Avoid when vocals are dense, bar length does not match, or the action requires missing cultural/song context.

source_library.typical_text: <member_name>!

vocal_chant.description: Vocal Chant is a rhythmcall action from the support library. Use it where the source context hints and duration requirements match; avoid using it as a generic filler.
vocal_chant.when_to_use: Best when section semantics, bar length, vocal density, and cultural context all match the source hints.
vocal_chant.when_not_to_use: Avoid when vocals are dense, bar length does not match, or the action requires missing cultural/song context.

source_library.typical_text: Follow the short chant or shouted phrase already present in the song.

sing_along.description: Sing Along is a rhythmcall action from the support library. Use it where the source context hints and duration requirements match; avoid using it as a generic filler.
sing_along.when_to_use: Best when section semantics, bar length, vocal density, and cultural context all match the source hints.
sing_along.when_not_to_use: Avoid when vocals are dense, bar length does not match, or the action requires missing cultural/song context.

source_library.typical_text: Sing the main hook or chorus melody with the crowd.
